title: goose: no logging on nftables drops
description: The nftables input and forward chains use 'policy drop' but have no logging rules before the implicit drop. This makes it difficult to debug why traffic is being dropped. Consider adding 'log prefix "nft-drop: " counter drop' as the final explicit rule in each chain, or at least for specific interface combinations. File: hosts/goose/services/firewall.nix.

title: pakhet: remove /etc/hosts entry for git.unixpimps.net
description:
pakhet has a manual /etc/hosts entry for git.unixpimps.net that works around the broken NAT hairpinning on the wired VLAN. Once dotfiles-skv (hairpinning fix) is deployed, this workaround should be removed.

Look for the hosts entry in pakhet's NixOS configuration (networking.extraHosts or similar) and remove it.

title: pakhet: remove legacy opsplaza module
description:
Remove the old JBoss-based opsplaza module (hosts/pakhet/services/opsplaza.nix) and all related infrastructure now that themailer-wrapper has replaced it.

## Context

The opsplaza service was the original JBoss-based themailer running in podman containers (CouchDB 2.3.1 + JBoss AS 7 + postfix relay). It has been replaced by themailer-wrapper, a Rust/axum rewrite with SQLite that runs as a single systemd service.

The old module is currently disabled (`services.opsplaza.enable = false` in default.nix) but all the code is still present.

## What to remove

1. **`hosts/pakhet/services/opsplaza.nix`** — delete entirely (podman containers, couchdb backup, borgbackup job, nginx vhost, tmpfiles, podman network)
2. **`hosts/pakhet/services/default.nix`** — remove `./opsplaza.nix` from imports, remove `services.opsplaza.enable = false;`
3. **`flake.nix`** — remove the `opsplaza` input (`tarball+https://git.unixpimps.net/ijohanne/opsplaza-artifacts/archive/master.tar.gz`), remove from outputs function parameters
4. **`flake.lock`** — will update automatically after removing the input
5. **`secrets/pakhet.yaml`** — consider removing `opsplaza_smtp_pass` if no longer needed (check: themailer-wrapper reuses it via sops template for SMTP credentials, so it may still be needed under that name)
6. **`scripts/migrate-opsplaza-couchdb.sh`** — delete the migration script
7. **`flake.nix` devShell** — remove `migrate-opsplaza-couchdb` and its `gsed-compat` helper from devShells.default packages
8. **On pakhet server** — clean up `/var/opsplaza/couchdb` data directory and `/var/backup/opsplaza` after confirming no longer needed
9. **Gitea** — archive or delete the `opsplaza-artifacts` repo on git.unixpimps.net

## Verification

- `nix flake check` passes
- pakhet deploys successfully
- `themailer.opsplaza.com` still works via themailer-wrapper
- No podman containers or images remain

title: chronos: install and configure nut_exporter for UPS monitoring
description:
## Summary

Install and configure DRuggeri's nut_exporter on chronos (Raspberry Pi, Debian) so the NUT UPS Grafana dashboard on goose can scrape metrics from both fatty and chronos.

The scrape config on goose is already deployed and targeting chronos-wired (10.255.101.202:9199). The Grafana dashboard (NUT UPS) is also deployed.

## Steps

1. **SSH to chronos via wired IP**
   ```bash
   ssh 10.255.101.202
   ```

2. **Verify NUT daemon is running**
   ```bash
   systemctl status nut-server
   upsc -l localhost
   ```
   Confirm the UPS name and that upsd is listening on 127.0.0.1:3493.

3. **Install nut_exporter**
   Download the linux/arm64 binary from GitHub releases:
   ```bash
   wget https://github.com/DRuggeri/nut_exporter/releases/latest/download/nut_exporter-linux-arm64
   chmod +x nut_exporter-linux-arm64
   sudo mv nut_exporter-linux-arm64 /usr/local/bin/nut_exporter
   ```

4. **Configure vars to export**
   Create a systemd service at `/etc/systemd/system/nut_exporter.service`:
   ```ini
   [Unit]
   Description=NUT Exporter for Prometheus
   After=nut-server.service

   [Service]
   ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/nut_exporter \
     --nut.vars_enable="battery.charge,battery.voltage,battery.runtime,input.voltage,input.voltage.nominal,output.voltage,output.frequency,ups.load,ups.status,ups.power,ups.power.nominal,ups.realpower,ups.beeper.status"
   Restart=always
   User=nobody

   [Install]
   WantedBy=multi-user.target
   ```

5. **Enable and start**
   ```bash
   sudo systemctl daemon-reload
   sudo systemctl enable --now nut_exporter
   ```

6. **Verify metrics are served**
   ```bash
   curl http://127.0.0.1:9199/ups_metrics
   ```
   Should return `network_ups_tools_*` metrics.

7. **Verify goose can scrape**
   From goose (or via ssh to goose):
   ```bash
   curl http://10.255.101.202:9199/ups_metrics
   ```

## Notes

- The exporter listens on port 9199 by default and serves UPS metrics at `/ups_metrics`
- Goose scrape config already targets chronos-wired (10.255.101.202:9199) with `instance="chronos"` label
- Must use the wired IP (chronos-wired), not WiFi — WiFi IP was unreachable from goose
- Same exporter and var list as fatty (FreeBSD pkg `sysutils/nut_exporter`)

title: goose: unbound intermittent SERVFAIL causes persistent macOS negative DNS caching
description:
## Problem

Unbound on goose intermittently returns SERVFAIL (displayed as NXDOMAIN by `host`) for valid domains. macOS aggressively caches these negative responses (300-3600s), causing prolonged DNS failures on clients even after unbound recovers.

## Observed behavior

- `host -t a comunidad.movistar.es 10.255.100.254` returns NXDOMAIN
- `host -t a comunidad.movistar.es 8.8.8.8` resolves fine
- After clearing macOS DNS cache (`sudo dscacheutil -flushcache; sudo killall -HUP mDNSResponder`), querying goose again succeeds — unbound now has the answer cached

## Root cause investigation (2026-03-09)

After deploy-goose restarts unbound, the cache is cold. Stats showed:
- 282 SERVFAIL out of 7061 queries (~4% failure rate)
- Trust anchor priming failure on startup: `failed to prime trust anchor -- could not fetch DNSKEY rrset . DNSKEY IN`
- 0 bogus DNSSEC responses, so likely not a validation issue

Suspected contributors:
1. **Cold cache after restart** — every query needs full recursion
2. **`use-caps-for-id`** (0x20 encoding) — CDN authoritative servers (CloudFront, Akamai) may not echo back randomized case, causing unbound to reject responses
3. **`qname-minimisation`** — can cause premature NXDOMAIN with some DNS hierarchies (known issue with `.es` domains)

## Mitigations applied (dcec8ff)

Added to `hosts/goose/services/dns.nix`:
- `cache-max-negative-ttl = 5` — cap negative response caching at 5 seconds
- `serve-expired-client-timeout = 1800` — wait 1.8s for recursion before returning stale/failed answer
- `log-servfail = yes` — permanently log SERVFAIL responses with domain names

## Next steps

- [ ] Monitor `journalctl -u unbound | grep SERVFAIL` after next deploy/restart to identify which domains fail and why
- [ ] If SERVFAILs persist, consider disabling `use-caps-for-id` or adding `caps-whitelist` for problematic domains
- [ ] If qname-minimisation is the cause, test with `qname-minimisation-strict: no` (should be default)
- [ ] Consider whether unbound restart during deploy can be avoided when config hasn't changed

title: goose: cloudflare-dyndns fails after deploy due to DNS cold cache
description:
## Problem

After `deploy-goose` restarts unbound, `cloudflare-dyndns.service` fails because all IP-check services are unreachable. The service can't resolve any of its HTTP endpoints during the DNS cold cache window:

- https://checkip.amazonaws.com/ — unreachable
- https://ipv4.icanhazip.com/ — unreachable  
- https://dynamicdns.park-your-domain.com/getip — unreachable

This causes the deploy itself to report failure (`switch-to-configuration` exit status 4) even though the actual system switch succeeded.

## Observed (2026-03-09)

```
Mar 09 19:25:53 goose cloudflare-dyndns-start: Service https://checkip.amazonaws.com/ unreachable, skipping.
Mar 09 19:25:53 goose cloudflare-dyndns-start: Service https://ipv4.icanhazip.com/ unreachable, skipping.
Mar 09 19:25:53 goose cloudflare-dyndns-start: Service https://dynamicdns.park-your-domain.com/getip unreachable, skipping.
Mar 09 19:25:53 goose cloudflare-dyndns-start: Tried all IP Services, but couldn't determine current IP address.
```

## Root cause

`cloudflare-dyndns.service` is triggered immediately when unbound restarts during deploy. With a cold DNS cache, the IP-check hostnames fail to resolve. The service has no retry/backoff — it tries all services once and exits with status 2.

## Possible fixes

1. Add `ExecStartPre` delay or `After=unbound.service` + a DNS readiness check
2. Add systemd restart-on-failure with a delay so it retries after cache warms
3. Use a systemd timer with `OnBootSec=60s` or similar to delay first run after boot/restart
4. Configure the dyndns client to use a fallback DNS resolver (e.g. 8.8.8.8) for its own lookups

## Files

- `hosts/goose/services/` — cloudflare-dyndns configuration (need to locate exact file)

title: goose: isolate camera and guest VLANs in nftables (input + forward)
description:
## Problem

Both the input and forward chains treat camera and guest VLANs as fully trusted:
- **Input**: blanket accept from all interfaces including guest/camera gives them access to every service on goose (SSH, Prometheus, Grafana, unbound-control, exporters, nginx)
- **Forward**: inter-VLAN accept rule lets cameras reach wifi/wired/mgnt and vice versa; cameras also get unrestricted internet access

## Design

### Input chain

Break up the blanket `iifname { wifi, wired, mgnt, guest, camera, ... } accept` into tiered access:

```nft
# Trusted: full access to goose
iifname { "wifi", "wired", "mgnt", "${interfaces.external}", "lo", "wg0" } counter accept

# Guest: DNS + DHCP only
iifname "guest" udp dport { 53, 67, 68 } accept
iifname "guest" tcp dport 53 accept
iifname "guest" ct state { established, related } accept
iifname "guest" drop

# Camera: DNS + DHCP only (camera→UNVR is same-VLAN, not routed through input)
iifname "camera" udp dport { 53, 67, 68 } accept
iifname "camera" tcp dport 53 accept
iifname "camera" ct state { established, related } accept
iifname "camera" drop
```

### Forward chain

**Inter-VLAN**: Remove camera and guest from the inter-VLAN accept rule:

```nft
# Trusted inter-VLAN only
iifname { "wifi", "wired", "mgnt", "${interfaces.external}", "wg0" } oifname {
  "wifi", "wired", "mgnt", "${interfaces.external}", "wg0" } counter accept

# Trusted → camera (for Protect web UI access from LAN)
iifname { "wifi", "wired", "mgnt", "wg0" } oifname "camera" counter accept

# Camera → trusted: denied (default drop)
```

**Internet access**: Restrict camera VLAN internet to UNVR only:

```nft
# Only UNVR gets internet from camera VLAN (firmware updates, cloud access)
iifname "camera" ip saddr ${network.hosts.unvr.ip} oifname { "ppp0", "${interfaces.external}", "mobile" } accept

# Guest keeps internet access (already in the blanket LAN→WAN rule, just keep guest there)
```

**Guest forwarding**: Guest keeps internet access but no inter-VLAN:

```nft
# Guest → internet only (keep in existing LAN→WAN rule)
iifname { "guest", "wifi", "wired", "mgnt", "${interfaces.external}", "wg0" } oifname {
  "ppp0", "${interfaces.external}", "mobile" } counter accept
```

## Summary

| Source | → goose (input) | → internet (forward) | → other VLANs (forward) |
|--------|-----------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| wifi/wired/mgnt/wg0 | full access | yes | yes (including → camera) |
| guest | DNS + DHCP only | yes | no |
| camera (UNVR) | DNS + DHCP only | yes | no |
| camera (all others) | DNS + DHCP only | no | no |

## Files

- `hosts/goose/services/firewall.nix` — input chain lines 24-33, forward chain lines 49-51 and 58-59

## Subsumes

- dotfiles-jby (guest/camera full access to router services)

title: goose: guest VLAN receives internal DNS search domains via DHCP
description:
## Problem

`hosts/goose/services/kea.nix:130-134` sets DNS search domains as a global DHCP option:

```nix
{
  code = 119;
  data = "local, ${network.domain}, unixpimps.net";
  name = "domain-search";
  space = "dhcp4";
}
```

This pushes `local`, `est.unixpimps.net`, and `unixpimps.net` as search domains to ALL subnets including guest (VLAN 150). Combined with DNS access to goose's unbound (which serves A records for all internal hosts), a guest can enumerate the internal network:

```bash
# Guest client can resolve internal hostnames
host fatty        # → 10.255.101.243
host pakhet       # → 10.255.101.200
host chronos      # → 10.255.100.202
host cloudkey     # → 10.255.254.240
```

This leaks internal host names, IP addresses, and network structure to untrusted clients.

## Fix

Move the search domain option from global to per-subnet config for trusted VLANs only. Guest and camera subnets should get no search domain or a neutral one.

### Step 1: Remove from global options

Remove the `domain-search` entry from the global `option-data` list (lines 130-134).

### Step 2: Add to each trusted subnet

Add to wifi (subnet id 1), wired (id 2), mgnt (id 5) subnet option-data:
```nix
{
  code = 119;
  data = "local, ${network.domain}, unixpimps.net";
  name = "domain-search";
  space = "dhcp4";
}
```

### Step 3: Guest and camera get no search domain

The guest subnet (id 3) and camera subnet (id 4) inherit no search domain — clients will use FQDN only.

## Relationship

Related to **dotfiles-9ug** (camera/guest VLAN isolation). That bead restricts firewall access; this bead restricts information leakage via DHCP. Both should be done but are independent — this is a DHCP config change, not a firewall change.

## Files

- `hosts/goose/services/kea.nix` — global option-data (lines 130-134), subnet configs (lines 176-328)

## Verification

After deploying:
```bash
# From a guest device, check DHCP lease:
# Search domain should be empty or not include est.unixpimps.net

# From a trusted device (wifi/wired):
# Search domain should still include est.unixpimps.net
host fatty  # should still resolve
```

title: goose: no 'ct state invalid drop' in nftables ruleset
description:
## Problem

The nftables ruleset has no rule to drop packets with invalid conntrack state. Invalid packets (nonsensical TCP flags, expired conntrack entries, unassemblable fragments, failed sequence number validation) are accepted by the blanket accept rules in both input and forward chains.

## Risk Assessment: LOW

### Why invalid conntrack packets matter
- TCP packets with nonsensical flag combinations (SYN+FIN, RST without a connection)
- Packets from connections that timed out of conntrack but remote side kept sending
- Fragments that can't be reassembled
- Packets that fail TCP sequence number validation

### Why this change is low risk

**Long-lived connections (SSH, migrations):**
TCP established conntrack timeout is 5 days (432000s). Any packet in either direction resets the timer. SSH keepalives (OpenSSH default ServerAliveInterval/TCPKeepAlive) and OS-level TCP keepalives (macOS default: every 2 hours) ensure active sessions never expire. A multi-day migration producing output has constant traffic — conntrack entry never gets close to expiring.

**Gaming (UDP/TCP):**
UDP conntrack stream timeout is 120s. Games send packets many times per second, resetting constantly. If a UDP "connection" does expire after 2+ minutes idle, the next packet creates a new conntrack entry (classified as "new", not "invalid") because UDP is connectionless. TCP game connections are short-lived (login, matchmaking) and always have traffic flowing.

**Flow offloading (currently disabled — see dotfiles-mnt):**
With offloading disabled, every packet goes through the full nftables chain. Conntrack sees everything consistently, so there's no risk of offloaded flows creating spurious "invalid" packets when falling back to software path. This makes the change even safer right now.

**WAN (ppp0/mobile):**
Already effectively protected — the `ct state { established, related }` rules on ppp0/mobile implicitly reject invalid packets. This change makes it explicit and adds protection for LAN interfaces too.

## Implementation

Add `ct state invalid counter drop` as the first rule in both input and forward chains:

```nft
chain input {
  type filter hook input priority filter; policy drop;
  ct state invalid counter drop
  ...existing rules...
}

chain forward {
  ...mss clamp...
  type filter hook forward priority filter; policy drop;
  ct state invalid counter drop
  ...existing rules...
}
```

The counter allows monitoring how many invalid packets are being dropped. If the count is unexpectedly high, it may indicate a misbehaving device or upstream issue.

## Files

- `hosts/goose/services/firewall.nix` — input chain (after line 18), forward chain (after line 45)

## Dependencies

- **dotfiles-mnt** (flow offloading kernel regression): When flow offloading is re-enabled, verify that `ct state invalid drop` doesn't interact badly with flows transitioning between offload and software path. Offloaded flows bypass nftables entirely, but when a flow falls back (e.g., conntrack timeout mismatch between offload table and nftables), packets could briefly appear as invalid. Monitor the counter after re-enabling offloading.

title: goose: ppp0 not in flowtable, no flow offload for PPPoE traffic
description: The flowtable 'fastnat' includes wifi, wired, camera, mgnt, and wan but NOT ppp0. Since the primary WAN is PPPoE (ppp0), flow offload does not accelerate the main internet traffic path. Adding ppp0 to the flowtable devices would enable hardware/software offload for PPPoE sessions. File: hosts/goose/services/firewall.nix, flowtable lines 65-68.

title: goose: SMTP port 25 open on WAN with no SMTP server
description: The nftables input chain accepts TCP port 25 from 0.0.0.0/0, including from ppp0/WAN. There is no SMTP server configured on goose. This is an unnecessary open port on the public internet. Either remove it or, if it was intended for forwarding, move it to the forward chain with a DNAT rule. File: hosts/goose/services/firewall.nix, input chain line 22.

title: goose: remove DNAT for cctax-node (now behind grpc-proxifier)
description:
## Problem

cctax-node is now exposed via grpc-proxifier, making the direct DNAT rules unnecessary and a security risk.

## Current state

DNAT rules are now declared per-host in `configs/network.nix` via `dnat` attributes (moved from hardcoded firewall rules in dotfiles-daj). cctax-node currently has:

```nix
cctax-node = { ip = "10.255.101.245"; mac = "58:9c:fc:03:64:32"; dnat = [
  { proto = "tcp"; port = 8888; }
  { proto = "tcp"; port = 20000; }
]; };
```

The `mkDnatRules` helper in `configs/network.nix` generates forward, prerouting, and preroutingLocal nftables rules from these attributes.

## Fix

Remove the `dnat` attribute from the `cctax-node` entry in `configs/network.nix`. This will automatically remove all three generated nftables rules (forward accept, prerouting DNAT, and local DNAT).

## Files

- `configs/network.nix`

title: goose: move DNAT port mappings from firewall.nix into configs/network.nix registry
goose: implement IPv6 ULA addressing for internal network
description:
Implement IPv6 Unique Local Addressing (ULA) across all VLANs using fd00::/8 prefix, enabling internal IPv6 communication without a public routable prefix. Internet traffic remains IPv4-only.

## ULA Scheme

Map existing VLAN structure to /64 subnets under fd00:255::/48:

| VLAN | IPv4 Subnet       | IPv6 ULA Subnet       | Gateway (goose)     |
|------|--------------------|-----------------------|---------------------|
|   35 | 10.255.35.0/24    | fd00:255:35::/64      | fd00:255:35::1      |
|  100 | 10.255.100.0/24   | fd00:255:100::/64     | fd00:255:100::1     |
|  101 | 10.255.101.0/24   | fd00:255:101::/64     | fd00:255:101::1     |
|  150 | 10.255.150.0/24   | fd00:255:150::/64     | fd00:255:150::1     |
|  200 | 10.255.200.0/24   | fd00:255:200::/64     | fd00:255:200::1     |
|  254 | 10.255.254.0/24   | fd00:255:254::/64     | fd00:255:254::1     |

## Scope

### networking.nix
- Keep `net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding = true`
- Add ULA addresses to each VLAN interface on goose
- Configure router advertisements (SLAAC) per interface so clients auto-configure

### nftables
- Add ip6 filter rules mirroring existing ip4 rules (input, forward, output)
- No ip6 NAT/masquerade (ULA is internal-only, no internet breakout)
- Block ip6 forwarding to WAN

### dns.nix (unbound)
- Add AAAA local-data records for all hosts
- Add ip6.arpa reverse zones and PTR records

### configs/network.nix
- Extend host registry with `ip6` field (or derive from IPv4 mapping)
- Extend helper functions to generate AAAA forwardDns and ip6 reverseDns

### kea (DHCP)
- Evaluate whether to add DHCPv6 or rely on SLAAC only
- SLAAC-only is simpler and sufficient for most use cases

## Notes
- ULA prefix fd00:255::/48 is not globally unique (RFC 4193 recommends random generation) but fine for a single-site LAN
- No changes needed for remote hosts (pakhet, khosu) unless they need IPv6 internal access

title: khosu: port 2525 exposed to internet unnecessarily
description:
## Problem

`hosts/khosu/services/postfix.nix:72` opens port 2525 on all interfaces:
```nix
networking.firewall.allowedTCPPorts = [ 25 80 2525 ];
```

Port 2525 is the relay submission port used exclusively by pakhet for outbound mail relay. Pakhet connects via WireGuard at `[10.100.0.8]:2525` (configured in `hosts/pakhet/services/mailserver.nix:36`). This traffic arrives on khosu's wg0 interface, not the public ens3 interface.

The postfix master.cf config for port 2525 (`hosts/khosu/services/postfix.nix:59-68`) restricts `mynetworks` to `10.100.0.0/24,10.255.0.0/16`, so an attacker can't relay mail through it. But it still accepts connections and responds to SMTP EHLO from any source on the internet, leaking service fingerprinting information.

## Fix

Add wg0 as a trusted interface and remove 2525 from public firewall:

```nix
networking.firewall = {
  allowedTCPPorts = [ 25 80 ];  # 25=inbound MX, 80=ACME
  trustedInterfaces = [ "wg0" ];  # all WireGuard traffic accepted
};
```

This means any future WireGuard-only services automatically work without opening ports publicly.

## Files

- `hosts/khosu/services/postfix.nix` — line 72, move port 2525 off public firewall
- `hosts/khosu/configuration.nix` — add trustedInterfaces (or in postfix.nix alongside existing firewall config)

## Verification

After deploying:
```bash
# From internet: should timeout/refuse
nmap -p 2525 khosu.unixpimps.net

# From pakhet via WireGuard: should connect
ssh pakhet.est.unixpimps.net "nc -z 10.100.0.8 2525 && echo ok"
```

title: goose: nftables forward chain rule ordering is unconventional
description: In the forward chain, the MSS clamp and flow offload rules appear before the 'type filter hook forward priority filter; policy drop;' declaration. While nftables is lenient about this, the type/hook/policy line should conventionally come first for readability. File: hosts/goose/services/firewall.nix, forward chain lines 44-46.

title: goose: test_connection script does not actually fail over
description:
## Problem

The `test_connection` script in `hosts/goose/configuration.nix` (lines 38-71) detects WAN failure correctly by fping-ing public DNS servers via ppp0 and mobile, but the failover branch just prints a message and exits 0. It never modifies the routing table.

## Current routing behavior

Goose has two default routes with different metrics:
```
default dev ppp0 scope link            # metric 0 (PPPoE, primary)
default via <gw> dev mobile metric 1063  # metric 1063 (DHCP, backup)
```

The kernel already fails over automatically when ppp0 disappears entirely (interface down). The gap is when **ppp0 is up but broken** — PPPoE session alive, ISP not routing traffic. The kernel still prefers ppp0 and mobile never kicks in.

## Implementation

Replace the current `test_connection` script with a stateful failover/failback script:

```bash
STATE_FILE="/var/run/wan-failover-state"
MAIN_WAN=ppp0
BACKUP_WAN=mobile
PROBE_TARGETS="8.8.8.8 8.8.4.4 1.1.1.1"
FPING_ARGS=(-c 3 -q -x 2)
FAILBACK_THRESHOLD=3  # consecutive successes before failing back

current_state() {
  cat "$STATE_FILE" 2>/dev/null || echo "primary"
}

failover_to_backup() {
  mobile_gw=$(ip route show dev mobile | awk '/default/{print $3}')
  ip route replace default via "$mobile_gw" dev mobile metric 0
  ip route replace default dev ppp0 scope link metric 9999
  echo "backup" > "$STATE_FILE"
  echo "0" > "${STATE_FILE}.failback-count"
  sms "WAN failover: ppp0 unreachable, switched to mobile"
  systemctl restart unbound.service
}

failback_to_primary() {
  mobile_gw=$(ip route show dev mobile | awk '/default/{print $3}')
  ip route replace default dev ppp0 scope link metric 0
  ip route replace default via "$mobile_gw" dev mobile metric 1063
  echo "primary" > "$STATE_FILE"
  rm -f "${STATE_FILE}.failback-count"
  sms "WAN failback: ppp0 restored"
  systemctl restart unbound.service
}

if [[ "$(current_state)" == "primary" ]]; then
  # Normal state: check if primary is down
  if ! fping -I "$MAIN_WAN" "${FPING_ARGS[@]}" $PROBE_TARGETS >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    if fping -I "$BACKUP_WAN" "${FPING_ARGS[@]}" $PROBE_TARGETS >/dev/null 2>&1; then
      failover_to_backup
    fi
  fi
else
  # Failed over: check if primary has recovered
  if fping -I "$MAIN_WAN" "${FPING_ARGS[@]}" $PROBE_TARGETS >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    count=$(cat "${STATE_FILE}.failback-count" 2>/dev/null || echo 0)
    count=$((count + 1))
    if [[ $count -ge $FAILBACK_THRESHOLD ]]; then
      failback_to_primary
    else
      echo "$count" > "${STATE_FILE}.failback-count"
    fi
  else
    echo "0" > "${STATE_FILE}.failback-count"
  fi
fi
```

### Key behaviors

- **Failover**: When ppp0 is up but not routing, swap default route metrics so mobile becomes primary
- **Failback**: Require 3 consecutive successful probes (3 minutes) before switching back to ppp0, preventing flap
- **SMS notification**: Uses existing `sms` script on both failover and failback
- **DNS**: Restarts unbound on both transitions to flush any cached negative responses from the broken WAN period
- **State tracking**: `/var/run/wan-failover-state` persists across checks but not across reboots (tmpfs), so a reboot always starts in primary state

### Systemd integration

Add a systemd timer running every 60 seconds:

```nix
systemd.services.wan-failover = {
  description = "WAN failover check";
  serviceConfig = {
    Type = "oneshot";
    ExecStart = "${test_connection}/bin/test_connection";
  };
};

systemd.timers.wan-failover = {
  wantedBy = [ "timers.target" ];
  timerConfig = {
    OnBootSec = "2min";
    OnUnitActiveSec = "1min";
  };
};
```

### runtimeInputs

Update `writeShellApplication` runtimeInputs to include: `bash fping iproute2 systemd sms`

## Files

- `hosts/goose/configuration.nix` — replace test_connection script (lines 38-71), add systemd timer/service

## Notes

- The existing `ppp/ip-up.d/10-post-up.sh` in networking.nix handles PPPoE reconnect (restarts unbound, smokeping-exporter, cloudflare-dyndns). This script handles the different case where PPPoE stays up but ISP stops routing.
- If both ppp0 and mobile are unreachable, the script does nothing — there's no working path to fail over to.
- Mobile gateway IP is dynamic (DHCP), so it's read from the routing table at failover time rather than hardcoded.

title: nftables flow offloading broken on kernel 6.19.5
description:
## Summary

nftables flowtable flow offloading broke on Linux kernel 6.19.5 (NixOS stable 25.11) due to a misconfiguration that was previously a harmless noop.

The flowtable `devices` list contained VLAN sub-interfaces (wifi, wired, camera, mgnt) instead of the underlying bond device (uplink). Since kernel 5.13, the flowtable discovers real netdevices behind VLANs and handles VLAN demuxing internally — the correct device to list is the bond/physical NIC, not VLAN sub-interfaces.

Before 6.19, the ingress hook on VLAN sub-interfaces never saw raw tagged frames, so the flowtable was effectively a noop. Kernel 6.19 changed hook ordering or VLAN processing, causing the misconfigured flowtable to actively break TCP connections (stalls during handshake).

## Fix

1. Changed flowtable devices from VLAN sub-interfaces to the bond device (`uplink`)
2. Re-enabled the `flow add @fastnat` rule in the forward chain
3. Disabled `hw-tc-offload` on both mlx5 NICs (not needed for software flowtable)

## Impact (while broken)

- All inter-VLAN traffic went through the full nftables chain (no fast-path)
- 1 Gbps PPPoE WAN unaffected (CPU handles 1G fine without offloading)
- 10G/25G inter-VLAN throughput was CPU-bottlenecked

title: goose: Prometheus admin API enabled unnecessarily
description:
## Problem

`hosts/goose/services/prometheus.nix:10` enables the Prometheus admin API:

```nix
extraFlags = [ "--web.enable-admin-api" ];
```

This exposes administrative endpoints on port 9001:
- `POST /api/v1/admin/tsdb/delete_series` — delete time series data
- `POST /api/v1/admin/tsdb/clean_tombstones` — clean up deleted data
- `POST /api/v1/admin/tsdb/snapshot` — create TSDB snapshots

Only reachable from trusted networks (wifi/wired/mgnt), so no direct internet exposure. But the admin API is not needed for normal operation — it's a maintenance tool that should be enabled on-demand if ever needed.

## Fix

Remove the flag:

```nix
# Remove this line entirely
extraFlags = [ "--web.enable-admin-api" ];
```

## Files

- `hosts/goose/services/prometheus.nix` — line 10

## Verification

After deploying:
```bash
# Should return 404 or disabled message
curl -s -X POST http://10.255.254.254:9001/api/v1/admin/tsdb/snapshot | head

# Normal scraping/querying still works
curl -s http://10.255.254.254:9001/api/v1/targets | head
```

title: pakhet: Roundcube bypasses TLS certificate verification for IMAP/SMTP
description:
## Problem

`hosts/pakhet/services/mailserver.nix:18-23` disables TLS peer verification for Roundcube's IMAP and SMTP connections:

```php
$config['imap_host'] = "ssl://localhost";
$config['smtp_host'] = "ssl://localhost";
$config['imap_conn_options'] = ['ssl' => ['verify_peer' => false, 'verify_peer_name' => false]];
$config['smtp_conn_options'] = ['ssl' => ['verify_peer' => false, 'verify_peer_name' => false]];
```

This was originally needed because the ACME cert is issued for `pakhet.est.unixpimps.net` (via `certificateScheme = "acme-nginx"` and `fqdn = "pakhet.est.unixpimps.net"` on line 46/87), which doesn't match `localhost`. At the time, NAT hairpinning wasn't working so connecting to the real hostname wasn't possible.

## Why it's now fixable

NAT hairpinning is working (fixed in 44573f2). DNS on goose resolves `pakhet.est.unixpimps.net` to `10.255.101.200` (pakhet's wired IP). Roundcube runs on pakhet itself, so connecting to `ssl://pakhet.est.unixpimps.net` resolves to its own IP, the ACME cert matches, and TLS verification passes.

## Fix

```nix
extraConfig = ''
  $config['imap_host'] = "ssl://pakhet.est.unixpimps.net";
  $config['smtp_host'] = "ssl://pakhet.est.unixpimps.net";
  $config['smtp_user'] = "%u";
  $config['smtp_pass'] = "%p";
'';
```

Remove the `imap_conn_options` and `smtp_conn_options` overrides entirely — default behavior (verify_peer = true) is correct.

## Prerequisite

Pakhet's DNS must resolve `pakhet.est.unixpimps.net` to its own IP. Currently `hosts/pakhet/configuration.nix:18` sets:
```nix
nameservers = [ "${network.hosts.goose.ips.wired}" ];
```
This points to goose (10.255.101.254) which resolves `pakhet.est.unixpimps.net` → `10.255.101.200`. Verified working.

## Files

- `hosts/pakhet/services/mailserver.nix` — lines 17-24

## Verification

After deploying:
```bash
# Test Roundcube can still send/receive
# Login to webmail.unixpimps.net, send a test email, verify inbox loads
```

title: Migrate pakhet PostgreSQL 14→18 for NixOS 25.11
description: Upgrade PostgreSQL on pakhet from 17 to 18 when NixOS 25.11 ships postgresql_18 in nixpkgs-stable. Current version is 14.22 (stateVersion 22.05), so a 14→17 upgrade is a prerequisite. Dependent services: screeny (3 instances), vardrun (2 instances), plausible. Add flake scripts (postgresql-upgrade-17-18-step{1,2,3}) for backup/preflight, migration, and post-upgrade verification.
implementation:
## Current State

- PostgreSQL **14.22** (default for `stateVersion = "22.05"`)
- Config: `hosts/pakhet/services/postgresql.nix` — just `services.postgresql.enable = true`
- No extensions configured, no custom PL/Python, no logical replication
- Data directory: `/var/lib/postgresql/14` (NixOS default)

## Dependent Services

- **screeny** — 3 instances (k111-agw, k111-test, k131-god) + geoip
- **vardrun** — 2 instances (unixpimps, opsplaza)
- **plausible** — analytics (also uses ClickHouse)

## Upgrade Path Considerations (14→15→16→17→18)

Even though pg_upgrade jumps directly 14→18, the accumulated breaking changes across all intermediate versions apply. Relevant items for pakhet:

### Critical

- **Data checksums (18)**: `initdb` now enables checksums by default. pg_upgrade requires matching checksum settings between old and new clusters. Since PG 14 almost certainly has checksums **disabled**, must pass `--no-data-checksums` to `initdb` for the new cluster, OR enable checksums on the old cluster first with `pg_checksums --enable` (requires downtime). Alternatively, take this opportunity to enable checksums on the new cluster and do a full `pg_dumpall`/restore instead of pg_upgrade.
- **MD5 password deprecation (18)**: PG 18 emits deprecation warnings for MD5 passwords. Check `SELECT rolname, rolpassword LIKE 'md5%' AS is_md5 FROM pg_authid WHERE rolpassword IS NOT NULL;` — if any roles use MD5, migrate to SCRAM-SHA-256 before or after upgrade (`SET password_encryption = 'scram-sha-256'; ALTER ROLE ... PASSWORD '...';`).
- **CREATEROLE privilege restrictions (16)**: CREATEROLE roles can no longer freely modify non-superuser roles. Verify screeny/vardrun/plausible service users have the privileges they need — their NixOS modules likely create roles via `ensureUsers`, which runs as superuser, so probably fine. Verify after upgrade.

### High

- **Search path security during maintenance (17)**: Functions used by expression indexes and materialized views must now specify `search_path` during creation. Check for expression indexes: `SELECT indexdef FROM pg_indexes WHERE indexdef LIKE '%(%';` — if any reference functions in non-default schemas, those functions need `SET search_path`.
- **FTS collation provider change (18)**: Full-text search now uses the cluster's default collation provider. After pg_upgrade, **reindex all FTS and pg_trgm indexes**: `REINDEX DATABASE <dbname>;` for each database.
- **AFTER triggers execute as queued role (18)**: Triggers now fire as the role active when the event was queued, not at execution time. If screeny/vardrun use AFTER triggers with role changes mid-transaction, behavior may differ.
- **VACUUM/ANALYZE on partitioned tables (18)**: Now processes inheritance children by default. Unlikely to matter for these services but worth noting for any manual maintenance.

### Medium

- **Logging defaults (15)**: `log_checkpoints` now defaults to `on`, `log_autovacuum_min_duration` defaults to 10 minutes. Expect noisier logs — configure log rotation if not already in place.
- **PUBLIC schema permissions (15)**: New databases no longer grant CREATE on `public` schema to PUBLIC. Existing databases are preserved on upgrade, but new databases created after upgrade follow the new default.
- **Generated columns default to VIRTUAL (18)**: If any service creates generated columns without specifying STORED, behavior changes. Existing STORED columns are unaffected.
- **pg_stat_wal view columns removed (18)**: If any monitoring queries reference `wal_write`, `wal_sync`, `wal_write_time`, `wal_sync_time` — they moved to `pg_stat_io`.
- **Renamed catalog columns (17)**: `pg_collation.colliculocale` → `colllocale`, `pg_database.daticulocale` → `datlocale`. If any monitoring or backup scripts reference these, update them.

### Low (unlikely to affect pakhet)

- Python 2 / PL/Python removal (15) — not used
- Exclusive backup mode removal (15) — borgbackup uses pg_basebackup
- `vacuum_defer_cleanup_age` / `promote_trigger_file` removal (16) — unlikely set
- `old_snapshot_threshold` / `adminpack` removal (17) — unlikely used
- `force_parallel_mode` → `debug_parallel_query` rename (16) — unlikely set

## Prerequisites

- PostgreSQL 18 must be available in nixpkgs-stable (expected NixOS 25.11)
- Verify borgbackup of `/var/backup/screeny` is current and restorable
- Check current checksum status: `sudo -u postgres pg_controldata | grep checksum`
- Check for MD5 passwords: `sudo -u postgres psql -c "SELECT rolname FROM pg_authid WHERE rolpassword LIKE 'md5%';"`

## Flake Scripts

Add `writeShellScriptBin` entries to the flake (x86_64-linux only), runnable via `nix run .#postgresql-upgrade-14-18-step<N>`.

### `postgresql-upgrade-14-18-step1` — Backup & preflight

```bash
set -euo pipefail

echo "=== Step 1: Backup and preflight check ==="

[[ $EUID -eq 0 ]] || { echo "Run as root"; exit 1; }

old=14
new=18

echo "Checking current checksum status..."
sudo -u postgres pg_controldata /var/lib/postgresql/${old} | grep -i checksum

echo ""
echo "Checking for MD5 passwords..."
sudo -u postgres psql -c "SELECT rolname, CASE WHEN rolpassword LIKE 'md5%' THEN 'MD5 (migrate to SCRAM!)' ELSE 'OK' END AS auth FROM pg_authid WHERE rolpassword IS NOT NULL;"

echo ""
echo "Checking for expression indexes..."
sudo -u postgres psql -At -c "SELECT schemaname || '.' || indexname || ': ' || indexdef FROM pg_indexes WHERE indexdef ~ '\(.*\('" 2>/dev/null || true

echo ""
echo "Checking for FTS indexes..."
sudo -u postgres psql -At -c "SELECT schemaname || '.' || indexname FROM pg_indexes WHERE indexdef LIKE '%tsvector%' OR indexdef LIKE '%gin%' OR indexdef LIKE '%gist%'" 2>/dev/null || true

echo ""
echo "Taking pg_dumpall backup..."
sudo -u postgres pg_dumpall > "/var/backup/postgresql-${old}-pre-upgrade-$(date +%Y%m%d).sql"
echo "Backup saved to /var/backup/"

echo ""
echo "Listing databases for reference..."
sudo -u postgres psql -l

echo ""
echo "Stopping PostgreSQL..."
systemctl stop postgresql.service

echo "Creating socket directory..."
mkdir -p /var/run/postgresql
chown postgres:postgres /var/run/postgresql

echo "Building pg_upgrade binaries..."
pg_old=$(nix-build --no-out-link '<nixpkgs>' -A postgresql_${old})
pg_new=$(nix-build --no-out-link '<nixpkgs>' -A postgresql_${new})

# CRITICAL: match checksum settings — PG18 enables checksums by default
# If old cluster has checksums disabled, we must disable on new cluster too
checksum_status=$(sudo -u postgres $pg_old/bin/pg_controldata /var/lib/postgresql/${old} | grep "Data page checksum" | awk '{print $NF}')
initdb_flags=""
if [ "$checksum_status" = "0" ] || echo "$checksum_status" | grep -qi "off\|disabled"; then
  echo "Old cluster has checksums DISABLED — passing --no-data-checksums to initdb"
  initdb_flags="--no-data-checksums"
fi

echo "Initializing new data directory..."
sudo -u postgres $pg_new/bin/initdb $initdb_flags -D /var/lib/postgresql/${new}

echo "Running pg_upgrade --check (dry run)..."
cd /var/lib/postgresql
sudo -u postgres $pg_new/bin/pg_upgrade \
  --socketdir=/var/run/postgresql \
  --old-bindir=$pg_old/bin \
  --new-bindir=$pg_new/bin \
  --old-datadir=/var/lib/postgresql/${old} \
  --new-datadir=/var/lib/postgresql/${new} \
  --check

echo ""
echo "=== Preflight passed. Proceed to step 2. ==="
echo "NOTE: PostgreSQL is stopped. If aborting, run: rm -rf /var/lib/postgresql/${new} && systemctl start postgresql.service"
```

### `postgresql-upgrade-14-18-step2` — Run pg_upgrade

```bash
set -euo pipefail

echo "=== Step 2: Run pg_upgrade ==="

[[ $EUID -eq 0 ]] || { echo "Run as root"; exit 1; }

old=14
new=18

pg_old=$(nix-build --no-out-link '<nixpkgs>' -A postgresql_${old})
pg_new=$(nix-build --no-out-link '<nixpkgs>' -A postgresql_${new})

echo "Ensuring PostgreSQL is stopped..."
systemctl stop postgresql.service 2>/dev/null || true

mkdir -p /var/run/postgresql
chown postgres:postgres /var/run/postgresql

echo "Running pg_upgrade..."
cd /var/lib/postgresql
sudo -u postgres $pg_new/bin/pg_upgrade \
  --socketdir=/var/run/postgresql \
  --old-bindir=$pg_old/bin \
  --new-bindir=$pg_new/bin \
  --old-datadir=/var/lib/postgresql/${old} \
  --new-datadir=/var/lib/postgresql/${new}

echo ""
echo "=== pg_upgrade completed. ==="
echo ""
echo "Next steps:"
echo "  1. Update hosts/pakhet/services/postgresql.nix to set package = pkgs.postgresql_18"
echo "  2. Commit, push, deploy-pakhet"
echo "  3. Run step 3 for post-upgrade verification"
```

### `postgresql-upgrade-14-18-step3` — Post-upgrade verification & cleanup

```bash
set -euo pipefail

echo "=== Step 3: Post-upgrade verification ==="

[[ $EUID -eq 0 ]] || { echo "Run as root"; exit 1; }

old=14
new=18

echo "Checking PostgreSQL service status..."
systemctl status postgresql.service --no-pager

echo ""
echo "Checking PostgreSQL version..."
sudo -u postgres psql -c "SELECT version();"

echo ""
echo "Listing databases..."
sudo -u postgres psql -l

# Apply extension updates if generated by pg_upgrade
if [ -f /var/lib/postgresql/update_extensions.sql ]; then
  echo ""
  echo "Applying extension updates..."
  sudo -u postgres psql -f /var/lib/postgresql/update_extensions.sql
fi

# Reindex for FTS collation provider change (PG18 requirement)
echo ""
echo "Reindexing all databases (required for FTS collation changes in PG18)..."
for db in $(sudo -u postgres psql -At -c "SELECT datname FROM pg_database WHERE datistemplate = false;"); do
  echo "  Reindexing $db..."
  sudo -u postgres reindexdb "$db" || echo "  WARNING: reindex of $db failed"
done

# Check MD5 passwords and warn
echo ""
echo "Checking for MD5 passwords (deprecated in PG18)..."
sudo -u postgres psql -c "SELECT rolname, CASE WHEN rolpassword LIKE 'md5%' THEN 'MD5 — MIGRATE TO SCRAM!' ELSE 'SCRAM (ok)' END AS auth FROM pg_authid WHERE rolpassword IS NOT NULL;"

# Run ANALYZE to refresh statistics after upgrade
echo ""
echo "Running ANALYZE on all databases..."
sudo -u postgres vacuumdb --all --analyze-only

echo ""
echo "Checking all dependent services are healthy..."
for svc in screeny-k111-agw screeny-k111-test screeny-k131-god screeny-geoip vardrun-unixpimps vardrun-opsplaza plausible; do
  status=$(systemctl is-active "$svc" 2>/dev/null || echo "not found")
  printf "  %-25s %s\n" "$svc" "$status"
done

echo ""
echo "=== Verification complete ==="
echo ""
echo "If everything looks good:"
echo "  1. Remove old data directory: rm -rf /var/lib/postgresql/${old}"
echo "  2. Delete pg_upgrade logs/scripts in /var/lib/postgresql/ (delete_old_cluster.sh, etc.)"
echo "  3. If MD5 passwords were found, migrate them to SCRAM-SHA-256"
```

## Step-by-step Procedure

1. **Update nixpkgs-stable** to a version that includes `postgresql_18`
2. **Build new config locally** — `nix build .#nixosConfigurations.pakhet.config.system.build.toplevel` with `services.postgresql.package = pkgs.postgresql_18;` to verify it compiles
3. **SSH to pakhet**, run `nix run .#postgresql-upgrade-14-18-step1` — audits current state, backs up, stops postgres, runs preflight
4. **Review preflight output** — pay attention to checksum handling, MD5 warnings, expression indexes
5. **If preflight passes**, run `nix run .#postgresql-upgrade-14-18-step2` — performs the actual migration
6. **Update `hosts/pakhet/services/postgresql.nix`** to set `services.postgresql.package = pkgs.postgresql_18;`, commit, push, deploy-pakhet
7. **Run `nix run .#postgresql-upgrade-14-18-step3`** — verify services, reindex (FTS), ANALYZE, apply extension updates
8. **Migrate MD5 passwords to SCRAM** if any were found
9. **Remove old data directory** once confirmed stable

## NixOS Config Change

```nix
# hosts/pakhet/services/postgresql.nix
{ pkgs, ... }:
{
  services.postgresql = {
    enable = true;
    package = pkgs.postgresql_18;
  };
}
```

## Rollback Plan

- The `pg_dumpall` backup from step 1 allows full restore to PostgreSQL 14
- The old data directory `/var/lib/postgresql/14` is preserved until explicitly deleted in step 3
- To rollback: revert the NixOS config change, deploy, stop postgres, restore old data dir, start postgres

## Reference

- https://www.monotux.tech/posts/2025/11/upgrading-postgresql-version-nixos/
- https://www.postgresql.org/docs/15/release-15.html
- https://www.postgresql.org/docs/16/release-16.html
- https://www.postgresql.org/docs/17/release-17.html
- https://www.postgresql.org/docs/18/release-18.html

title: Remove trivial home-manager wrapper files
description: Several host-specific home-manager files only import a user module and add no host behavior. Collapse these into flake-level host metadata or a clearer user-plus-overrides pattern to reduce file sprawl.

Replace unbound with hickory-dns on goose
description:
Replace unbound DNS resolver on goose with hickory-dns (Rust-based authoritative + recursive DNS server) to gain DDNS support for DHCP clients via Kea D2.

## Motivation

- Native RFC 2136 dynamic DNS update support (TSIG-authenticated), enabling automatic DNS registration for DHCP clients
- Rust-based, memory-safe DNS server backed by ISRG/Prossimo
- Built-in Prometheus metrics (no separate exporter needed)
- We already use hickory-dns as a resolver library in other Rust projects

## Approach: Option A — DDNS for unknown clients only

Static zone records generated from `configs/network.nix` remain the source of truth for known hosts. DDNS via Kea D2 only adds records for unknown/guest clients that get dynamic DHCP leases. Kea D2's `check-with-dhcid` conflict resolution ensures static records (no DHCID) are never overwritten.

## Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: hickory-dns package with prometheus-metrics feature
- Override/overlay nixpkgs hickory-dns (0.25.2) or package 0.26.0-beta.2
- Enable `prometheus-metrics` and `recursor` cargo features
- Decide: stay on 0.25.2 (stable, in nixpkgs) or take 0.26.0-beta.2 (TSIG-only auth, better recursor, SOA serial persistence fix)
- Recommendation: 0.26.0-beta.2 — TSIG is the only auth Kea D2 speaks, SOA serial fix is critical for DDNS

### Phase 2: Zone file generation from configs/network.nix
- Extend network.nix helpers to generate BIND-format zone files instead of (or in addition to) unbound local-data entries
- Generate forward zone: `est.unixpimps.net` (A/AAAA records, SOA, NS)
- Generate reverse zones: `in-addr.arpa` for each /24 subnet (PTR records, SOA, NS)
- IPv6 reverse zones if enableIPv6ULA is set

### Phase 3: hickory-dns TOML configuration on goose
- Authoritative primary zones for est.unixpimps.net + reverse zones (from generated zone files)
- Recursor zone for `.` (recursive resolution for everything else)
- TSIG key configuration for Kea D2 authentication
- SqliteAuthority backend for zones that accept DDNS updates
- Prometheus endpoint on localhost (e.g., 127.0.0.1:9000)
- Listen on all interfaces, ACL matching current unbound setup (10.0.0.0/8, 127.0.0.0/8)

### Phase 4: Kea D2 (DHCP-DDNS) integration
- Enable kea-dhcp-ddns service on goose
- Configure forward DDNS domain (est.unixpimps.net) and reverse DDNS domains
- TSIG key shared between Kea D2 and hickory-dns
- Conflict resolution: check-with-dhcid (default) — won't touch static records

### Phase 5: Monitoring
- Add scrape config in hosts/goose/services/monitoring/hickory-dns.nix
- Build Grafana dashboard (hosts/goose/services/grafana-dashboards/hickory-dns.json):
  - Query rate (by type, protocol, opcode)
  - Response codes (NOERROR, NXDOMAIN, SERVFAIL)
  - Cache hit/miss rate and latency histograms
  - Cache size, in-flight queries
  - DDNS update activity (zone_records_modified_total)
  - Process metrics (CPU, memory, FDs)
- Remove unbound exporter and dashboard after cutover

### Phase 6: Cutover
- Test build: `nix build .#nixosConfigurations.goose.config.system.build.toplevel`
- Run hickory-dns on alternate port for parallel testing
- Validate: forward resolution, reverse resolution, recursive queries, DDNS updates
- Switch: replace unbound with hickory-dns in goose config
- Remove unbound configuration and exporter
- Update cloudflare-dyndns service dependency (unbound.service → hickory-dns.service)
- Update failover hooks that restart unbound → restart hickory-dns

## Key files affected
- `hosts/goose/services/dns.nix` — replace unbound config with hickory-dns
- `hosts/goose/services/kea.nix` — add D2 (DHCP-DDNS) configuration
- `configs/network.nix` — add zone file generation helpers
- `hosts/goose/services/monitoring/unbound.nix` → `hickory-dns.nix`
- `hosts/goose/services/grafana-dashboards/unbound.json` → `hickory-dns.json`
- `hosts/goose/services/cloudflare.nix` — update service dependency
- `hosts/goose/networking.nix` — update failover hooks
- `flake.nix` — possibly add hickory-dns overlay/package

## Version notes
- nixpkgs has 0.25.2; 0.26.0-beta.2 is latest (March 2026)
- 0.26 removes SIG(0), TSIG-only for dynamic updates (matches Kea D2)
- 0.26 fixes SOA serial persistence after RFC 2136 updates (critical for DDNS)
- 0.26 has major recursor improvements (negative caching, DNSSEC, connection pooling)
- NixOS module (services.hickory-dns) is incomplete — manual TOML config via configFile is the way

comments.author: Ian Johannesen
comments.text: Phase 3 updated: bind to explicit IPs instead of wildcards to work around hickory-dns/hickory-dns#3401 (wrong source address on UDP responses from wildcard-bound sockets on multi-homed hosts). Goose has one address per VLAN interface so the bug likely wouldn't manifest today, but explicit binding is the correct fix and avoids relying on that assumption.

comments.author: Ian Johannesen
comments.text: Implementation complete across all 6 phases. Key decisions: used 0.25.2 with feature overrides (not 0.26 beta), skipped TSIG auth for now (ACL-only), added grafana/printcam/obico to extraDns to compensate for losing unbound transparent zone behavior. All hosts evaluate cleanly. Ready for test build and deployment.

implementation:
## Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: hickory-dns package with prometheus-metrics feature
- Override/overlay nixpkgs hickory-dns (0.25.2) or package 0.26.0-beta.2
- Enable `prometheus-metrics` and `recursor` cargo features
- Decide: stay on 0.25.2 (stable, in nixpkgs) or take 0.26.0-beta.2 (TSIG-only auth, better recursor, SOA serial persistence fix)
- Recommendation: 0.26.0-beta.2 — TSIG is the only auth Kea D2 speaks, SOA serial fix is critical for DDNS

### Phase 2: Zone file generation from configs/network.nix
- Extend network.nix helpers to generate BIND-format zone files instead of (or in addition to) unbound local-data entries
- Generate forward zone: `est.unixpimps.net` (A/AAAA records from network.nix, SOA, NS — static only, no DDNS writes)
- Generate forward zone: `dhcp.est.unixpimps.net` (empty, DDNS-only — for unknown clients on trusted wifi/wired VLANs)
- Generate forward zone: `guest.est.unixpimps.net` (empty, DDNS-only — for guest VLAN clients)
- Generate reverse zones: `in-addr.arpa` for each /24 subnet (PTR records, SOA, NS)
- IPv6 reverse zones if enableIPv6ULA is set

### Phase 3: hickory-dns TOML configuration on goose
- Authoritative primary zones:
  - `est.unixpimps.net` — static records from network.nix only, no DDNS writes accepted
  - `dhcp.est.unixpimps.net` — DDNS-only zone for unknown clients on trusted VLANs (wifi, wired)
  - `guest.est.unixpimps.net` — DDNS-only zone for guest VLAN clients
  - Reverse zones for each /24 subnet
- Recursor zone for `.` (recursive resolution for everything else)
- **Separate TSIG keys** per zone so each D2 forward domain can only write to its designated zone
- SqliteAuthority backend for DDNS zones (dhcp.est.unixpimps.net, guest.est.unixpimps.net)
- File-based authority for est.unixpimps.net (static, regenerated on rebuild)
- Prometheus endpoint on localhost (e.g., 127.0.0.1:9000)
- **Bind to explicit IPs, not wildcards** — generate `listen_addrs_ipv4` / `listen_addrs_ipv6` from `network.hosts.goose.ips` + loopback, rather than using `0.0.0.0` / `::0`. This works around [hickory-dns/hickory-dns#3401](https://github.com/hickory-dns/hickory-dns/issues/3401): hickory-dns doesn't use `IPV6_PKTINFO`, so wildcard-bound UDP sockets can send responses from the wrong source address on multi-homed hosts. Goose's one-address-per-interface layout likely avoids the symptom today, but explicit binding is the correct fix and future-proofs against additional addresses.
- ACL matching current unbound setup (10.0.0.0/8, 127.0.0.0/8, fc00::/7, ::1/128)

### Phase 4: Kea D2 (DHCP-DDNS) integration
- Enable kea-dhcp-ddns service on goose
- **Zone isolation by trust level:**
  - Reserved hosts (all VLANs): `ddns-override-client-update = true` — Kea uses reservation hostname, DDNS updates match static records in est.unixpimps.net (effectively no-ops since IP matches)
  - Unknown clients on wifi/wired VLANs: `ddns-qualifying-suffix = "dhcp.est.unixpimps.net"` — writes to dhcp.est.unixpimps.net only
  - Guest VLAN: `ddns-qualifying-suffix = "guest.est.unixpimps.net"` — writes to guest.est.unixpimps.net only
  - No client can poison est.unixpimps.net static records
- **DHCP search domain option (option 119):**
  - Reserved hosts (mgmt, camera, and reserved wifi/wired): `est.unixpimps.net, unixpimps.net`
  - Unknown clients on wifi/wired: `dhcp.est.unixpimps.net, est.unixpimps.net, unixpimps.net`
  - Guest VLAN: `guest.est.unixpimps.net` (only — guests cannot resolve short names in est.unixpimps.net or unixpimps.net via search domain)
- Configure reverse DDNS domains per subnet
- **Conflict resolution: `no-check-with-dhcid`** on all VLANs (including guest)
  - Allows records to move immediately when a device gets a new MAC/IP (e.g., Apple MAC randomization) without waiting for old lease expiry
  - For unknown clients: last device to claim a hostname wins, which is the desired behavior
  - Hostname collisions between different unknown devices are rare on a home network
- **DDNS TTL: 60-120 seconds** on all VLANs (including guest)
  - This is local-only recursive DNS serving a handful of LAN clients, so extra query load from short TTLs is negligible
  - Stale records resolve correctly almost immediately after IP changes
- **Do NOT change DHCP lease times** — all VLANs stay at current values (24h valid-lifetime, 36h max). DDNS TTL handles staleness independently of lease duration.

### Phase 5: Monitoring
- Add scrape config in hosts/goose/services/monitoring/hickory-dns.nix
- Build Grafana dashboard (hosts/goose/services/grafana-dashboards/hickory-dns.json):
  - Query rate (by type, protocol, opcode)
  - Response codes (NOERROR, NXDOMAIN, SERVFAIL)
  - Cache hit/miss rate and latency histograms
  - Cache size, in-flight queries
  - DDNS update activity (zone_records_modified_total)
  - Process metrics (CPU, memory, FDs)
- Remove unbound exporter and dashboard after cutover

### Phase 6: Cutover
- Test build: `nix build .#nixosConfigurations.goose.config.system.build.toplevel`
- Run hickory-dns on alternate port for parallel testing
- Validate: forward resolution, reverse resolution, recursive queries, DDNS updates
- Validate: unknown wifi/wired client DDNS writes only to dhcp.est.unixpimps.net
- Validate: guest device DDNS writes only to guest.est.unixpimps.net
- Validate: guest search domain does not include est.unixpimps.net or unixpimps.net
- Validate: reserved host search domain is est.unixpimps.net, unixpimps.net only
- Switch: replace unbound with hickory-dns in goose config
- Remove unbound configuration and exporter
- Update cloudflare-dyndns service dependency (unbound.service → hickory-dns.service)
- Update failover hooks that restart unbound → restart hickory-dns

## Key files affected
- `hosts/goose/services/dns.nix` — replace unbound config with hickory-dns
- `hosts/goose/services/kea.nix` — add D2 (DHCP-DDNS) configuration, per-subnet ddns-qualifying-suffix, updated search domain options
- `configs/network.nix` — add zone file generation helpers (incl. dhcp.est.unixpimps.net, guest.est.unixpimps.net)
- `hosts/goose/services/monitoring/unbound.nix` → `hickory-dns.nix`
- `hosts/goose/services/grafana-dashboards/unbound.json` → `hickory-dns.json`
- `hosts/goose/services/cloudflare.nix` — update service dependency
- `hosts/goose/networking.nix` — update failover hooks
- `flake.nix` — possibly add hickory-dns overlay/package

## Version notes
- nixpkgs has 0.25.2; 0.26.0-beta.2 is latest (March 2026)
- 0.26 removes SIG(0), TSIG-only for dynamic updates (matches Kea D2)
- 0.26 fixes SOA serial persistence after RFC 2136 updates (critical for DDNS)
- 0.26 has major recursor improvements (negative caching, DNSSEC, connection pooling)
- NixOS module (services.hickory-dns) is incomplete — manual TOML config via configFile is the way

implementation:
## Recommended order
3. Do after `dotfiles-ng-j40` if you want one consistent module-arg story for host-level shared context.

## Goal
Extract repeated remote-host setup into a shared module for managed servers.

## Suggested approach
- Create a shared module under `configs/` or `configs/profiles/` for remote hosts that need:
  - `nix.extraOptions` with `nix_builder_access_tokens`
  - common `sops.age` settings
  - standard deploy script installation

title: Pass shared host context via module args instead of import wrappers
description: Replace repeated patterns like importing modules with { inherit network interfaces; } arguments by pushing shared context through specialArgs or _module.args. This should make imports plain module paths, reduce boilerplate, and make host service trees easier to refactor.
implementation:
## Recommended order
2. Do after `dotfiles-ng-5qh` if you touch flake wiring at the same time, or independently if you want a narrower refactor.

## Goal
Stop threading `network`, `interfaces`, and similar values through nested `(import ./module.nix { ... })` wrappers.

## Suggested approach
- Pass shared values once via `specialArgs` or `_module.args` at the host/module entrypoint.
- Convert child imports back to plain paths wherever possible.
- Apply first in high-churn trees like `hosts/goose/services` and `hosts/goose/services/monitoring`.
- Prefer module arguments for host-local context and reserve explicit imports for true parameterized module factories.

## Done when
- Service trees read mostly as plain `imports = [ ./foo.nix ./bar.nix ];`.
- Shared host context is defined once near the top of the host graph.
- There is less repeated `inherit network` / `inherit interfaces network` boilerplate.

title: Set up PostgreSQL exporter and Grafana dashboard for pakhet
description:
Set up pgsty/pg_exporter on pakhet to monitor PostgreSQL, and create a comprehensive Grafana dashboard on goose.

## Context

- PostgreSQL runs on **pakhet** (currently PG 14, will be upgraded per dotfiles-ng-73j)
- Prometheus + Grafana run on **goose**
- No PostgreSQL monitoring currently exists
- Use **pgsty/pg_exporter** v1.2.0 (Go, ~600+ metrics, YAML config) — NOT the standard prometheus-postgres-exporter
- pg_exporter is not in nixpkgs — package it in **nur-packages** first

## Scope

### 1. Package pg_exporter in nur-packages (`../nur-packages`)

#### Package: `pkgs/pg-exporter/default.nix`
- Use `buildGoModule` from `github:pgsty/pg_exporter` v1.2.0
- Install the bundled `config/` YAML collector directory to `$out/share/pg_exporter/`

#### Module: `modules/pg-exporter/default.nix`

NixOS service module. pg_exporter ships 54 bundled collector YAML files with complex version-aware SQL queries — these should be used as defaults rather than re-expressed in Nix. The module combines bundled collectors with declarative Nix-defined overrides:

- `enable`, `package` (default to the pg-exporter package)
- `port` (default 9630), `listenAddress` (default `"0.0.0.0"`)
- `environmentFile` — systemd EnvironmentFile for `PG_EXPORTER_URL` and other secrets
- `defaultCollectors` (bool, default true) — include bundled collector YAMLs from the package
- `disabledCollectors` (list of strings) — bundled collector names to skip (sets `skip: true`)
- `settings` — freeform Nix attrset rendered to YAML via `pkgs.formats.yaml`. Defines custom or overridden collectors. Merged into the config directory alongside bundled configs. This is the declarative Nix → YAML path for any pakhet-specific collectors.
- `autoDiscovery` (bool) — enable `--auto-discovery` to monitor all databases
- `extraFlags` — additional CLI flags
- `enableLocalScraping` — add Prometheus scrape config (same pattern as hue/nftables exporters)

The module builds a config directory: bundled YAMLs (if `defaultCollectors`) + a generated YAML from `settings`, then passes `--config <dir>` to pg_exporter. Disabled collectors are handled by generating a YAML file that redefines them with `skip: true`.

Systemd service with DynamicUser, ProtectSystem/ProtectHome hardening.
Expose in `flake.nix` as `nixosModules.pg-exporter`.

### 2. Wire up in dotfiles-ng

Add `ijohanne-nur.nixosModules.pg-exporter` import in `flake.nix`.

#### Initial exporter config: `hosts/pakhet/services/pg-exporter.nix`

```nix
{ config, ... }:
{
  sops.secrets.pg-exporter-env = {
    sopsFile = ../secrets.yaml;
  };

  services.pg-exporter = {
    enable = true;
    port = 9630;
    listenAddress = "0.0.0.0";
    autoDiscovery = true;
    environmentFile = config.sops.secrets.pg-exporter-env.path;

    # Use all bundled collectors except irrelevant ones
    defaultCollectors = true;
    disabledCollectors = [
      # No pgbouncer on pakhet
      "pgbouncer_list"
      "pgbouncer_database"
      "pgbouncer_stat"
      "pgbouncer_pool"
      # No TimescaleDB
      "pg_tsdb_hypertable"
      # No Citus
      "pg_citus"
      # No replication/subscriptions (single primary)
      "pg_recv"
      "pg_sub"
      "pg_origin"
      "pg_pubrel"
      "pg_subrel"
      "pg_sync_standby"
      "pg_downstream"
      # Heartbeat not needed (requires pg_heartbeat extension)
      "pg_heartbeat"
    ];

    # Custom collectors can be added here in pure Nix → YAML, e.g.:
    # settings = {
    #   my_custom_collector = {
    #     name = "my_custom_collector";
    #     desc = "Custom query for pakhet";
    #     query = "SELECT ...";
    #     ttl = 30;
    #     tags = [ "cluster" ];
    #     metrics = [
    #       { my_metric = { usage = "GAUGE"; description = "..."; }; }
    #     ];
    #   };
    # };
  };
}
```

The sops secret `pg-exporter-env` contains:
```
PG_EXPORTER_URL=postgresql:///postgres?host=/run/postgresql&sslmode=disable
```

This connects via the local Unix socket (no password needed for peer auth). The `autoDiscovery` flag makes it automatically find and monitor all databases (screeny-k111-agw, screeny-k111-test, screeny-k131-god, vardrun-unixpimps, vardrun-opsplaza, plausible).

#### Active bundled collectors (what we get with the above config):

| Category | Collectors | What they monitor |
|----------|-----------|-------------------|
| Core | `pg`, `pg_meta`, `pg_setting` | Version, uptime, recovery status, GUC settings |
| Activity | `pg_activity`, `pg_wait`, `pg_backend`, `pg_xact`, `pg_lock`, `pg_query` | Connections by state/db, wait events, transaction rates, lock distribution, query stats |
| Database | `pg_db`, `pg_db_confl` | Per-database TPS, tuple ops, temp files, deadlocks, conflicts |
| Tables/Indexes | `pg_table`, `pg_index`, `pg_func`, `pg_seq`, `pg_relkind`, `pg_defpart` | Seq/index scans, live/dead tuples, function calls, sequences |
| Size & Bloat | `pg_size`, `pg_table_size`, `pg_table_bloat`, `pg_index_bloat` | Database sizes, table sizes, bloat estimates |
| I/O & Storage | `pg_io`, `pg_archiver` | I/O stats by backend type, WAL archiver status |
| WAL & Checkpoint | `pg_wal`, `pg_bgwriter`, `pg_checkpointer`, `pg_checkpoint` | WAL generation, bgwriter/checkpointer buffers, checkpoint timing |
| Replication | `pg_repl`, `pg_slot` | Replication status, slot lag (active slots only, safe even without replicas) |
| Maintenance | `pg_vacuuming`, `pg_indexing`, `pg_clustering`, `pg_backup` | In-progress vacuum/index/cluster/backup operations |
| Internal | `pg_ssl`, `pg_timeline`, `pg_recovery`, `pg_slru`, `pg_shmem` | SSL connections, timeline info, recovery state, SLRU/shmem stats |
| Wait Events | `pg_wait_event` | Detailed wait event distribution |

### 3. Add Prometheus scrape job on goose

Add `pg-exporter` scrape target in `hosts/goose/services/prometheus.nix`:
- Target: `pakhet:9630`
- Job name: `postgres`

### 4. Create comprehensive Grafana dashboard

Add `postgres.json` to `hosts/goose/services/grafana-dashboards/`.
Dashboard panels (leverage pg_exporter's metric names prefixed `pg_`):

- **Overview**: uptime, version, connected databases, total connections, server info
- **Connections**: active, idle, idle in transaction, waiting, by database, by state (`pg_activity_*`)
- **Transaction rates**: commits/s, rollbacks/s, TPS by database (`pg_db_xact_commit`, `pg_db_xact_rollback`)
- **Query performance**: rows fetched/returned/inserted/updated/deleted per second (`pg_db_tup_*`)
- **Cache**: buffer cache hit ratio (`pg_db_blks_hit / (pg_db_blks_hit + pg_db_blks_read)`), bgwriter stats
- **Locks**: lock counts by mode, waiting locks, deadlocks (`pg_lock_count`, `pg_db_deadlocks`)
- **Table stats**: seq scans vs index scans, live/dead tuples, table bloat (`pg_table_*`, `pg_table_bloat_*`)
- **Index stats**: index usage ratio, unused indexes, index bloat (`pg_index_*`, `pg_index_bloat_*`)
- **WAL**: WAL generation rate, WAL size (`pg_wal_*`)
- **Vacuum**: autovacuum activity, tables needing vacuum (`pg_table_n_dead_tup`, `pg_vacuuming_*`)
- **Database sizes**: total size, per-database sizes, growth trends (`pg_size_*`, `pg_db_size`)
- **Temp files**: temp file usage by database (`pg_db_temp_*`)
- **Checkpoints**: checkpoint frequency, write/sync time, buffers (`pg_checkpoint_*`, `pg_checkpointer_*`)

## Notes

- pg_exporter default port: 9630
- Config format: YAML — bundled collectors for defaults, `pkgs.formats.yaml` for custom Nix-defined collectors
- Key env var: `PG_EXPORTER_URL` (connection string)
- Key flags: `--config`, `--auto-discovery`, `--web.listen-address`
- Health endpoints: `/up`, `/health`, `/primary`, `/replica`

title: pakhet: unencrypted IMAP and POP3 ports exposed to internet
description:
## Problem

`hosts/pakhet/services/mailserver.nix:42-44` enables unencrypted IMAP and POP3:

```nix
enablePop3 = true;      # port 110
enablePop3Ssl = true;   # port 995
enableImapSsl = true;   # port 993
enableImap = true;       # port 143
```

These unencrypted ports are DNAT'd from goose's WAN to pakhet via `hosts/goose/services/firewall.nix:79`:
```nix
tcp dport { 25, 80, 110, 143, 443, 465, 587, 993, 995, 2525, 4190 } dnat ${network.hosts.pakhet.ip};
```

This means ports 110 and 143 are internet-facing. Credentials sent over unencrypted IMAP/POP3 are visible in plaintext. All mail clients should use the SSL variants (993/995).

Roundcube already uses `ssl://` connections (ports 993/465), so disabling unencrypted ports does not affect webmail.

## Fix

### pakhet mailserver.nix

```nix
enablePop3 = false;     # disable port 110
enableImap = false;      # disable port 143
enablePop3Ssl = true;   # keep port 995
enableImapSsl = true;   # keep port 993
```

### goose firewall.nix

Remove ports 110 and 143 from both the DNAT prerouting rule and the forward chain accept rule:

Line 63 (forward):
```nix
tcp dport { 25, 80, 443, 465, 587, 993, 995, 2525, 4190 } ct state new accept
```

Lines 79-80 (prerouting DNAT):
```nix
tcp dport { 25, 80, 443, 465, 587, 993, 995, 2525, 4190 } dnat ${network.hosts.pakhet.ip};
```

Also update the `fib daddr type local` hairpin DNAT rule on line 80 to match.

## Files

- `hosts/pakhet/services/mailserver.nix` — lines 42-44
- `hosts/goose/services/firewall.nix` — lines 63, 79, 80

## Verification

After deploying both pakhet and goose:
```bash
# From internet: should refuse/timeout
nmap -p 110,143 pakhet.est.unixpimps.net

# SSL ports still work
openssl s_client -connect pakhet.est.unixpimps.net:993 </dev/null
openssl s_client -connect pakhet.est.unixpimps.net:995 </dev/null

# Test existing mail clients still connect (must use SSL)
```

## Notes

Verify no mail clients are still using unencrypted IMAP/POP3 before deploying. Check dovecot logs for connections on port 110/143.

title: goose: NAT hairpinning broken for wired VLAN (same-subnet as pakhet)
description:
NAT hairpinning fails when a client on the wired VLAN (10.255.101.0/24) tries to access services via the WAN IP. It works fine from other VLANs (wifi, etc).

**Root cause:** Missing masquerade for same-subnet hairpinned traffic in `hosts/goose/services/firewall.nix`.

When a wired client (10.255.101.x) hits goose's WAN IP on port 443, goose DNATs to pakhet (10.255.101.200). But postrouting line 86 has no action:
```
oifname { "wifi", "wired", "camera", "mgnt" }
```
So the packet arrives at pakhet with the original client source IP. Since pakhet and the client are on the same L2 segment, pakhet responds directly to the client, bypassing goose. The client drops the response because the source IP (10.255.101.200) doesn't match the WAN IP it connected to.

**Fix:** Add masquerade for DNAT'd traffic re-entering the wired VLAN:
```
oifname "wired" ip daddr 10.255.101.200 ct status dnat masquerade
```
This ensures pakhet sees goose's wired IP (10.255.101.254) as source, routes the reply back through goose, and goose un-DNATs + un-SNATs correctly.

**File:** `hosts/goose/services/firewall.nix` line 86

title: goose: ICMP accepted from all sources without rate limiting
description:
## Problem

The nftables input chain accepts all ICMP unconditionally (`ip protocol icmp accept` on line 21 of firewall.nix). This rule sits before the stateful ppp0/mobile drop rules, so WAN sources can send arbitrary ICMP to goose itself (floods, redirects, source-quench, etc.).

## Scope

**Input chain only** — traffic destined to goose itself. Forwarded traffic (LAN clients pinging the internet) is unaffected; those packets go through the forward chain where `ct state established,related` handles replies after conntrack de-NAT.

## Implementation

Replace line 21 (`ip protocol icmp accept`) with:

```nft
# LAN: accept all ICMP (PMTUD, redirects, diagnostics)
ip saddr 10.0.0.0/8 ip protocol icmp accept

# WAN: always allow PMTUD-essential types (blocking breaks path MTU discovery)
iifname { "ppp0", "mobile" } icmp type { destination-unreachable, time-exceeded, parameter-problem } accept

# WAN: rate-limit echo-request (ping)
iifname { "ppp0", "mobile" } icmp type echo-request limit rate 5/second burst 10 packets accept

# WAN: drop all other ICMP (redirects, source-quench, etc.)
iifname { "ppp0", "mobile" } ip protocol icmp drop
```

## Files

- `hosts/goose/services/firewall.nix` — input chain, line 21

## Notes

- destination-unreachable, time-exceeded, parameter-problem must always be accepted from WAN or PMTUD breaks
- echo-request rate-limited to 5/sec burst 10 — enough for diagnostics, prevents flood
- No changes to forward chain — LAN-to-WAN ICMP forwarding remains unrestricted
